name: The Hero's Journey
shortDescription: A narrative archetype detailing a hero's adventure from ordinary life to a transformative journey and eventual return. It emphasizes trials, growth, and the acquisition of wisdom or power.
description: While the specific phrase "The Hero's Journey" is a modern concept popularized by Joseph Campbell, the underlying narrative structure is evident in many ancient myths, including Sumerian stories. The journey typically involves a call to adventure, refusal of the call, crossing a threshold into the unknown, facing trials and tribulations, receiving help from mentors or allies, confronting a central crisis, achieving a reward or boon, and finally returning home transformed. In Sumerian mythology, the Epic of Gilgamesh exemplifies aspects of the hero's journey. Gilgamesh, initially an arrogant king, embarks on a quest for immortality after the death of his friend Enkidu. This quest takes him through dangerous lands and tests his strength and resolve. Although he doesn't achieve literal immortality, he gains wisdom and understanding about the human condition and his role as king, returning to Uruk a changed and more responsible ruler. The Sumerian version emphasizes themes of mortality, friendship, the limitations of human power, and the importance of civilization. The journey is not merely physical but also internal, leading to self-discovery and a deeper understanding of the world. The related domains of Kingship, Heroic friendship, The quest for immortality, Wisdom through suffering, and Civilization vs. wilderness are all key elements found within the Sumerian iteration of the Hero's Journey, showing how the hero's actions and experiences shape not only themselves but also the world around them.

sources.title: Mythology: Timeless Tales of Gods and Heroes
sources.author: Edith Hamilton

sources.title: The Hero With a Thousand Faces
sources.author: Joseph Campbell
